description: Operating systems internals - memory, processes, scheduling, I/O.

facts.context: When a page fault occurs, the OS:
facts.truth: loads the page from disk to RAM on demand
distractors: terminates the process immediately with an error
allocates additional physical RAM to the system
swaps out the entire process to make room

facts.context: Kernel preemption allows the scheduler to:
facts.truth: interrupt kernel code to run higher priority tasks
distractors: allow user processes to directly modify kernel memory
disable all hardware interrupts globally for safety
run the system without any process scheduler at all

facts.truth: shares pages until a write occurs
distractors: copies all parent memory immediately to the child
never copies any memory pages between processes
only works for processes marked as read-only

facts.context: Spinlocks are preferred over mutexes when:
facts.truth: critical section is short avoiding context switch
distractors: the critical section takes a long time to execute
the lock must work across different processes
deadlock detection is required by the application

facts.truth: access file data through memory operations
distractors: access network files faster than local files
automatically compress file contents transparently
encrypt file contents without explicit calls
only incrementing a process counter variable
recompiling the process code for the new CPU
copying all process memory to new locations

facts.truth: high priority task waits for low priority holding lock
distractors: priorities are assigned in the wrong numerical order
the scheduler runs in reverse priority order
all tasks have the same priority level assigned
recently accessed file data from the disk
network packet headers for fast routing
compiled code for frequently called functions

facts.context: A counting semaphore differs from mutex by:
facts.truth: allowing multiple concurrent accesses up to count
distractors: being strictly binary with only two states possible
requiring the same thread to release as acquired
having no blocking capability for waiting threads

facts.context: DMA (Direct Memory Access) improves I/O performance by:
distractors: using the CPU to move each byte individually
compressing data before transfer to reduce size
encrypting all data transfers automatically

facts.context: Hardware interrupts vs polling - interrupts are better when:
facts.truth: events are infrequent reducing CPU overhead
distractors: events occur at a constant predictable rate always
the CPU has no other work to perform meanwhile
latency requirements are extremely strict

facts.truth: mutual exclusion, hold-wait, no preemption, circular wait
distractors: only mutual exclusion and circular wait together
any two of the four Coffman conditions present
infinite resources and infinite processes only
